coredata.dc:title: Multi-functional carbon dots from simple precursors: An excellent heavy metal ions sensor with photocatalytic activity in aqueous environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Annamalai, Arun
Annamalai, Kumaresan
Ravichandran, Ramya
Bharathkumar, S.
Elumalai, Sundaravadivel
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with core and surface structures were prepared from simple precursors (ethylenediamine and ethylene glycol) by using house-hold microwave oven. The structural and optical properties were exclusively studied with HR-TEM, XRD, UV-Vis and PL techniques. The fluorescence emission of the CDs was predominantly dependent and/or independent on excitation-wavelength are owed to the existence of assorted functional groups on surface and uniform arrangement in core structure. Hence, the CDs shows better stability against photo bleaching and ionic strength. In addition, CDs are successfully used to detect heavy metal ions such as Cr6+ and Hg2+ selectively by fluorescence-based detection method through different quenching mechanisms with lower detection limit of 0.09 µM for Cr6+ and 0.16 µM for Hg2+. Similarly, we attempted rapid, simple and low cost on-field application by using two different cellulose-based filter papers for visual detection of Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal ions for quick and cheap method. Moreover, CDs were successfully employed for detoxification/degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) dye and also 87 % degradation were achieved over the time period of 140 min under direct sunlight irradiation. At finally, the innovative CDs are superior multi-behavior material for the several environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Multi-behavior
Dual heavy metal ions
Visual detection
Sunlight active

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protocol for recognition of heavy metal ions
Detection of toxic heavy metal ions
Interference study of other metal ions with CDs and specific metal ion detection
Simple and rapid paper-based detection probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) HR-TEM image of CDs with size distribution histogram. (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs (d-f) XPS high resolution spectrum of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s.
(a) UV-Vis absorption of CDs (b) Emission spectrum at different wavelength of CDs (c) Normalized spectrum of CDs at different wavelength with core and surface emission representation.

(a) PL spectra of CDs at increasing Cr6+ concentrations (b) plot of corresponding PL response (I0/I) vs Cr6+ concentration and (c) Linear fit (I0/I). (d) Emission spectra of CDs at different Hg2+ concentrations (e) plot of analogous PL response (I0/I) vs Hg2+ concentration and (f) Linear fit (I0/I) respectively.

(a) UV-Vis absorption of CDs with Cr6+ followed by excitation and emission of CDs, (b) UV-Vis absorption of CDs with Hg2+ followed by excitation and emission of CDs and (c) Illustration of possible mechanism of CDs quenching by Cr6+ and Hg2+.
(a-b) CDs + Cr6+ in low grade and Whatman FP (c-d) CDs + Hg2+ in low grade and Whatman FP.
(a-b) UV-Vis absorbance of RhB corresponding C/C0 plot. (c) C/C0 plot of various radical scavengers and (d) kinetic plot for degradation of RhB dye and with different radical scavengers.
Schematic representation of probable formation of CDs.

head.ce:title: Multi-functional carbon dots from simple precursors: An excellent heavy metal ions sensor with photocatalytic activity in aqueous environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu, 603203, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering Physics, College of Engineering, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation Vaddeswaram, Andra Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Engineering Physics, College of Engineering, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation Vaddeswaram

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering Physics, College of Engineering, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation Vaddeswaram, Andra Pradesh, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with core and surface structures were prepared from simple precursors (ethylenediamine and ethylene glycol) by using house-hold microwave oven. The structural and optical properties were exclusively studied with HR-TEM, XRD, UV-Vis and PL techniques. The fluorescence emission of the CDs was predominantly dependent and/or independent on excitation-wavelength are owed to the existence of assorted functional groups on surface and uniform arrangement in core structure. Hence, the CDs shows better stability against photo bleaching and ionic strength. In addition, CDs are successfully used to detect heavy metal ions such as Cr6+ and Hg2+ selectively by fluorescence-based detection method through different quenching mechanisms with lower detection limit of 0.09 µM for Cr6+ and 0.16 µM for Hg2+. Similarly, we attempted rapid, simple and low cost on-field application by using two different cellulose-based filter papers for visual detection of Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal ions for quick and cheap method. Moreover, CDs were successfully employed for detoxification/degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) dye and also 87 % degradation were achieved over the time period of 140 min under direct sunlight irradiation. At finally, the innovative CDs are superior multi-behavior material for the several environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are the promising, carbonaceous fluorescent, zero-dimensional, eco-friendly ultra-small material with sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon core and functionalized surface structure [1]. CDs have many important qualities such as less toxic, low-cost preparation, water solubility, and tunable fluorescence from UV to NIR region making CDs a more promising futuristic material. It is broadly studied in numerous fields such as photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, toxic metal ions, molecular sensors, etc., [2–4]. However, among the researchers, the complete mechanism of multiple emissive states in CDs is still a question. Various researchers reported that the three different mechanisms for multiple emissions are due to molecular state, quantum confinement, and surface state. But the major number of reports agrees with multiple emission from CDs are based on the composition of the core, molecular and surface state [5,6]. K. C. Lin group reported that the multiple emissive states of carbon dots originate from the core, molecular/surface state of multi-functionalized carbon dots made with citric acid and ethylenediamine [7]. The synthesis of facile CDs from very simple precursors leads to make a better understanding to elucidate the fluorescence mechanism of the CDs.

Heavy metals are one of the major pollutants which are carcinogenic to human beings, living things, and the whole environment [8]. When these heavy metal ions enter into our environment, it can affect our surface water and underground water levels very rapidly. Among the heavy metals, Cr6+ and Hg2+ are well-known, non-biodegradable and highly toxic to all living things and aquatic environments [9]. Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions are mostly released to the aquatic environment through industrial and agricultural processes. Hg2+ is a highly poisonous substance because of its non-biodegradable and good water solubility. Owing this property, Hg2+ can be transformed into methyl mercury (highly toxic) by microorganisms present in water, which can easily affect our whole ecosystem through the food chain system resulting in a serious effect on human organs like kidney, lungs, central nervous system, and cardiovascular system, etc. [9,10]. On the other side, the chromium ions in water shows Cr6+ and Cr3+ oxidation states, among these two ions, Cr6+ ions are more poisonous to our living community, which affects many human organs even in a trace amount of accumulation [11]. Hence, it is very necessary to detect toxic metals in an aqueous environment by using low-cost, time-effective methods and probes material.

Many fluorescence-based probes like metal nano-clusters, organic molecules, and quantum dots are available [12]. Apart from the above-mentioned probes, CDs are one of the most efficient and eco-friendly materials for the recognition of metal ions in an aqueous environment compared to other fluorescent probes material [13]. Carbon dots, are a carbonaceous material that can be prepared by a simple route with comparably good fluorescence quantum yield and low toxic nature [14]. The special ability of CDs can detect more than one metal ion due to their well and multi-functionalized surface, which marks the CDs as a unique material. Numerous methods are reported in the detection of metal ions such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, and electrochemical and Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) techniques [15]. Among the above methods CDs, based fluorescence sensors are unique because of their rapid detection, probe preparation, high selectivity, and sensitivity [16].

Apart from the detection, detoxification of water pollutants is very important for the current situation. The ground and surface waters are highly affected by the contaminated dyes released from various industries. The unusable organic dyes released to water bodies could cause serious damage to the aquatic and all living systems through the food chain, in addition, the synthetic dyes in the aquatic system can decrease the dissolved oxygen system [17,18]. Therefore, to overcome this problem, various techniques were developed by the researchers which are photo catalysis, membrane filtration, etc. Among these techniques, photocatalytic method is the easiest, cost-effective and efficient in outdoor process [19]. CDs are the most effective and less-toxic, photoactive semiconductor material and also has the capacity to eliminate the organic pollutants with the help of light source.

Here, we aimed to study diverse properties of CDs, so that we synthesized highly fluorescent CDs from simple precursors like ethylenediamine and ethylene glycol without any addition of solvent through a single-step microwave aided method. The as-synthesized material emits bright blue-color fluorescence at 425 nm used for the recognition of multiple hazardous heavy metal ions such as Cr6+ and Hg2+ then followed by photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant (RhB). Among the various metal ions tried, Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal ions show good fluorescence quenching responses and follows different quenching mechanism like inner-filter effect, and excited-state electron transfer mechanisms. Here we reported that, instrumental based sensor, and made a low-cost visual fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of multiple heavy metal ions in aqueous medium with two different cellulose-based filter papers for better comparison. Furthermore, the CDs looks a potential candidate to remove the toxic organic dye RhB in aqueous environment with the help of direct sunlight and also exhibits the degradation efficiency of about 87 % over the time period of 140 min. To the best of our knowledge, there are very few works reported with utilizing carbon dots for detecting metal ions and sunlight assisted photocatalytic degradation applications in single work.

All the reagents used in this work were purchased from SRL Pvt Ltd. All the reagents received and used as without any further purification. Only milli-Q water was used for the entire experimental studies.

Absorption spectra were recorded with the help of Carry-60 UV-Visible spectrometer. Photo luminescent emissive measurements taken from Horiba Fluoromax R928P. FTIR spectral measurements were analyzed by using Shimadzu IR Tracer 100 with ATR mode. Surface properties collected from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ULVAC-PHI 5000 probe Ⅲ XPS). Zeta potential carried with Horiba particle size analyzer SZ-100. XRD pattern analyzed with PAN analytical Aeris bechtop X-ray diffractometer furnished with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) as X-ray source. The morphological and structural properties were studied with high resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100 Plus HR-TEM).

The blue emissive CDs prepared via facile one-stage microwave treatment by using ethylenediamine (0.016 M) and ethylene glycol (0.03 M) total 3 ml are taken in a 50 ml beaker, without using any solvent, then mixed gently to make better solubility. The solution placed in a domestic Panasonic microwave oven 700 W for 7 min, yields dark brown color solid product (CDs). The solid product was cooled to room temperature and the solid brown product was dispersed with 20 ml of milli-Q water. Finally, the brown color solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm followed by filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane syringe filter, and the resultant solution was deposited at room temperature and used for further studies.

Quantum yield for the as-synthesized CDs was found that 33 %, for that quinine sulphate in 0.1 M sulphuric acid were used as a reference standard for quantum yield calculation of CDs. For quantum yield calculation, both the QS and CDs were prepared below the absorbance value of 0.1 at their corresponding exciting wavelength followed by the emission intensity recorded with 360 nm for QS and 350 nm for CDs and the subsequent quantum yield was calculated from the Eq. (1).
(1) ΦCDs = φQS × ICDs/IQS × AQS/ACDs× ηCDs 2/ηQS 2
Where, QS is quinine sulphate. CDs is carbon dots. I correspond to integrated PL intensity and A is absorbance and finally η is refractive index for both QS and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protocol for recognition of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the ability of prepared CDs fluorescence response against various metal ions was examined by fluorescence spectroscopic technique in the aqueous environment. For recognition of dual metal ions using CDs, all the metal ion solutions were prepared from their respective metal salts. Initially, a stock solution of 1 mM concentrated solution of metal ions solution made into 10 ml with Milli-Q water after that 100 µL of CDs in 3 ml of Milli-Q water taken in a cuvette as a probe solution for metal ions detection. To the probe solution, known concentration of metal ions/heavy metal ions, were added. After the addition of metal ions to the probe solution, right away the fluorescence intensity of the solution was quenching for Hg2+ or Cr6+ ions, the emission intensity was recorded at 425 nm (excited at 350 nm) immediately without any post treatment after the addition of CDs. To study the effect of interference from other metal ions for selectivity, to the mixture of known concentration of Hg2+ or Cr6+ and the known concentrations of other individual metal ions are further added to the CDs probe solution and their corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were noted under same experimental conditions were followed for individual metal ions detection and the fluorescence emission quenching plot were plotted as a bar graph. Interference of other metal ions while detection of Hg2+ or Cr6+ and other metal ions (200 µM) and common anions (300 µM) are further added to the CDs solution and their fluorescence responds are recorded at the same experimental conditions were followed for all the other metal ions and the fluorescence respond were plotted as error bar diagram for better comparison of CDs selectivity and sensitivity.

For the visual detection of Cr6+and Hg2+ metal ions without any additional supporting agents, fluorescent filter paper probe was prepared from cellulose-based filter paper with different cost such as low-grade cellulose filter paper and Whatman filter paper. Initially, Whatman filter papers were cut into the size of 1.5 × 4 cm, and half of the filter paper was dipped into freshly prepared CDs solution for 2 s, after the paper was air-dried at room temperature. The same procedure was repeated for another cellulose based low-grade filter paper, for a better evaluation the paper pieces were dipped into CDs solution in landscape direction.

The photocatalytic performance of CDs against carcinogenic water effluents were extensively studied under the irradiation of sunlight. Initially, 50 ml of 20 ppm dye solutions were prepared freshly to that 1 ml of CDs solution was added. The whole solution was stirred at dark condition to attain adsorption-desorption equilibrium after 20 mins, the whole mixture set up was kept under direct sunlight in open atmosphere between the time of 10.30 AM to 12.50 PM at SRM institute of science and technology, Kattankulathur campus, Chennai. The samples collected from the reaction mixture at different time breaks of 20 min. The degradation efficiency of the dye molecule was studied with the help of the absorbance spectrum, the collected samples were recorded by using UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

X-ray diffraction spectrum indicates the amorphous nature of CDs (Figure. S1) the broad peak centered at 23.42° relates to (002) plane value, confirms the presence of irregular arrangement of graphitic structure. Furthermore, HR-TEM images of CDs ( Fig. 1a-b) were appeared as quasi-spherical in shape with the average particle size of 5 nm with d-spacing value of 0.33 nm.

The primary surface chemical analysis was performed by recording FT-IR spectra to determine the functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1c). CDs shows a broad peak located at the range of ~3300 cm−1 belonging to the –OH and –NH stretching vibration of hydroxyl and amine groups [20] and a small peak at 2130 cm−1 and 1280 cm1 resembles to stretching vibration of nitrile and carbonyl groups [21]. The sharp peak positioned at ~1640 cm−1 belongs to CC stretching [22]. FT-IR spectrum confirms the presence of the CC aromatic C-CDs structure and rich hydroxyl and amine groups present on the exterior of S-CDs (carbon dots surface) is formed during the carbonization process. The surface state of CDs was further deeply studied with X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) for the deep investigation of elemental confirmation and surface functional studies. The XPS survey spectra of CDs (Figure. S2a) show binding energies in different ranges at 284 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV equivalent to the C, N, and O separately. The elemental composition shown by the spectra are carbon (80.3 %), nitrogen (6.8 %), and oxygen (12.9 %). High-resolution C1s spectrum deconvoluted into multiple peaks (Fig. 1d), the major peak at 284.7 eV shows the presence of CC [23], followed by shoulder peaks to be found at 285.4 eV and 288.3 eV corresponds to C-N and CO groups on the surface.

The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) reveals the two bonding states at 399.6 eV, 398.1 eV, which matches to –NH2 and 398.1 eV due to pyridinic N groups [24] being present in CDs. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) show peaks at 531.7 eV and 533.4 eV are owing to the presence of C-O and OC-O* groups respectively [25]. From the C1s, N1s, and O1s survey spectrum and deconvoluted spectrum, it is revealed that carbon dot’s structure constructed with C-CDs assembled with the contribution of O and N elements followed by the surface composed with multiple functional groups with occupied negative charge about (−0.9 mV) on the surface confirmed through the zeta potential analysis (Figure. S2b). The results obtained from XPS, zeta potential and FT-IR represent that the CDs are formed with a carbon core and multiple functional groups on the surface with negative charge.

The optical response of as-synthesized CDs was studied with the UV-Vis absorption technique. The absorption spectra of CDs exhibit a very good wide absorption range at 345 nm and 270 nm (
Fig. 2a). The absorption at 270 nm is ascribed due to the π-π * transition [26,27]of the homogenous carbonic core of aromatic sp2 hybridized CC in the C-dots, followed by the broad absorption centered at 345 nm resulting from n-π * transition [26] of multiple functional groups present on the S-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in aqueous solution were recorded by continuously changing the excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence spectrum shows that the emission spectrum purely depends on the excitation-wavelength which results multiple-range emission properties. While exciting at 280 nm there were two different and comparable peaks attained with an emission peak at 330 nm with more intensity followed by a less intense peak at 425 nm, further increasing the excitation range up to 470 nm, the peak at 330 nm was gradually disappeared with an upturn in emission peak at 425 nm with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and further intensity decreases towards red shift (Fig. 2b). These results show that the maximum emission at 330 nm and 425 nm is corresponds to the presence of C-CDs (Carbon dots core) and S-CDs (Carbon dots surface) represented in ( Scheme 1). The emission from the S-CDs is more intense than the C-CDs, it might be the rate of electron-hole pair recombination in the C-CDs structure being lower when compared to the S-CDs [7]. Moreover, it is exciting to note that the fluorescence emission spectra obtained at 330 nm corresponds to C-CDs which are purely excitation wavelength-independent, on the other hand the emission spectrum obtained from the S-CDs at 425 nm which exhibits excitation-dependent in nature shown in (Fig. 2c), which was owing to the structure of CDs composed of even energy level with uniform C-CDs structure, uniform arrangement with contribution of N and O molecules (pictured in graphical abstract in Scheme 1) confirmed by XPS analysis. And there is a difference in energy levels on the S-CDs owing to presence of manifold functional groups like –NH, -CN, -CO, and –OH with lone pair of electrons which may be reduce the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO and leads to their higher recombination rate.

To investigate the stability of as-synthesized CDs against diverse environments like the effect of fluorescence stability against ionic strength with the different concentration of NaCl (Figure. S3a) UV light irradiation under 365 nm (Fig. S3b), temperature (Fig. S4b). However, the CDs with the increasing ionic environment and temperature variance the corresponding emission intensity does not affect greatly, followed by under UV irradiation also not much difference obtained, but there were very small gradual decreases in intensity of CDs corresponds to light irradiation time that may be due to that the emission from CDs at 425 nm belongs to S-CDs, direct recombination of electron-hole pair takes place on the S-CDs [28], so S-CDs was not much hindered against continuous photobleaching. S-CDs show a variation under the different range of pH (2−11) using HCl-NaOH system (Fig. S4e), the fluorescence intensity of CDs was increases from lower to higher pH condition. The reason may due to, the CDs are composed with rich in amine based functional group. So, under acidic pH condition the electrons are present in the amine groups are not able to localize freely due to -NH2 group became NH3 + ions, but in basic condition, the basic environment -NH2 group became more electron-rich which further helps for localization of free electrons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of toxic heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing capability of CDs with various metal ions under an aqueous environment was also investigated. Several metal ions are added individually to the CDs probe in an aqueous environment and its corresponding fluorescence variations were recorded. Particularly efficient quenching of fluorescence was noticed with the addition of Cr6+
Fig. 3a) and Hg2+ (Fig. 3d). However, there is no significant changes was noticed in fluorescence intensity with other metal ions were plotted as bar graph and visually noted (Fig. S4a & g). For the detection of heavy metal ions, the concentration of metal ions used in the range from 3 to 50 µM for Cr6+ and 3–150 µM Hg2+ respectively and plotted against I0/I.

The recorded emission spectra have shown the reduction in fluorescence intensity at 425 nm with mounting concentration of Hg2+ ions, a plot of fluorescence intensity (I0/I) versus Hg2+ concentration was plotted. From the plot, a linear relation of (R2 = 0.9803) was observed from the recognition range of 13–150 µM (Fig. 3f). Although the CDs probe shows a better fluorescence quenching response with Cr6+ compared to Hg2+, in existence of Cr6+ the fluorescence intensity of the CDs probe was completely quenched, which can be observed through naked eyes, the decreased fluorescence intensity at 425 nm, a plot of fluorescence intensity (I0/I) versus Cr6+ concentration was plotted. Linear relation of (R2 = 0.9957) was observed from 6.5 to 50 µM (Fig. 3c) of detection range with the help of linear correlation equation. The calculated Stern-Volmer quenching constant for Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions are 0.029 × 106 L mol−1 and 0.030 × 106 L mol−1 respectively. The calculated values were found with change in intensity with increase in concentration of metal ions was fitted by using Stern-Volmer equation [29].
(2) I0/I = 1 + Ksv[Q]
Where, I0 and I are the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at 425 nm in absence and presence of metal ions. Q represents the concentration of analyte and Ksv corresponds to quenching constant.

The above studies exhibit CDs, a very good effective probe for selective detection of heavy metal ions like Cr6+ and Hg2+ individually in the aqueous environment. Therefore the lower limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the formula of 3σ/m [13] for Cr6+ and Hg2+ is 0.09 µM and 0.16 µM was found from the linear fit plot of I0/I for Cr6+ and Hg2+ respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study of other metal ions with CDs and specific metal ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of the Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions were explained in the experimental part, the error bar in (Figure. S4b&d) shows I0/I value corresponds to the PL quenching. Another error bar graph shows the emission intensity of CDs at 425 nm, after the addition of various metal ions and common anions in presence of Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions with CDs were plotted as comparative bar graph and common anions (Figure. S4a&c) and temperature (Figure. S4h). From the obtained results, there were no other metal cations, anions and temperature interfered during the detection process. This would help to detect both Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions individually in the unknown solutions. In the study of pH influence (Figure. S4f), at acidic pH the detection ability of the CDs became reduced as mentioned above the -NH2 group on the surface of CDs was preferred to exist as -NH3 +, which directly affect the interaction with metal ions and leads to affect the detection process slightly.

From the studies of FT-IR, XPS, and zeta potential, it is confirmed to that the S-CDs is covered by electron-rich functional groups like amine, alcohol, etc., which makes a better interaction (electrostatic interaction) with metal ions. However, to explore the fluorescence turning off / Quenching mechanism by hazardous dual metal ions in an aqueous environment.

We studied the possibilities of different fluorescence quenching mechanisms, like inner filter effect (IFE), Photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and energy transfer mechanisms [30]. To study the possible mechanism for the detection of dual metal ions, initially we monitored if there is emission from Cr6+ and Hg2+ under UV light illumination, but there is no emission arises, which ruled out the possibility of the FRET process [31]. The transformation of UV-Vis absorption spectral changes of CDs by the addition of hazardous heavy metal ions was monitored. Interestingly there were observable changes occurred in the UV-Vis absorption spectrum, while the addition of Cr6+ to CDs in aqueous solution, a slight increase in absorption peak at both the range of 270 nm and 345 nm with constant addition of Cr6+ (Figure. S5a). After the constant addition of Cr6+ ions to CDs, it can reduce the chance for excitation of CDs and steadily decreases the emission of CDs with increases the addition of Cr6+ ions. However fine overlapping of CDs excitation spectra and absorption spectra of CDs with Cr6+ followed by partially overlap of emission spectrum ( Fig. 4a) are favors the possibility of the inner-filter effect [32], the reason for quenching of CDs fluorescence by Cr6+ ions byre absorbing the emission of CDs and also, we checked if there is an interaction between the CDs and Cr6+ with the help of FT-IR spectrum but there is no considerable changes noticed in FT-IR spectrum corresponds to CDs - Cr6+ complex (Figure. S5c). Furthermore, there is a constant decrease in absorption on the addition of Hg2+ ions to CDs (Figure. S5b), from this case, inner-filter effect was eliminated in fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ ions [31]. Followed by the quenching of fluorescence were combined with shift in emission spectrum towards the red from 425 nm to 438 nm may owing to the transfer of electron from CDs to Hg2+ in exited state (Fig. 4d). Therefore, there will be a possibility of the excited-state electron transfer process takes place from CDs to Hg2+ ions direct to quenching of fluorescence intensity, by therefore the fluorescence quenching is caused by the PET mechanism on the excited state [31].

Furthermore, while the addition of Hg2+ to CDs there was no evidence for formation of complex between CDs and metal ions in ground state was confirmed with FT-IR spectrum, which supports that the quenching of emission from CDs by Hg2+ is by transfer of electron from CDs to Hg2+ on the excited state (Figure. S5d). Finally, the CDs are very responsible to Cr6+ and Hg2+ detection with two different quenching mechanisms are explained and picturized in (Fig. 4b) and previously reported LOD values are tabulated in (Table S1&S2) for Cr6+ and Hg2+ individually.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simple and rapid paper-based detection probe
ce:section.ce:para: Simple, rapid and visual detection of metal ions with low cost, eco-friendly method without any sophisticated analytical techniques, filter paper was used as visual detection probe for the made-up CDs @ FP (filter paper) were dipped into the prepared aqueous solution of Cr6+ and Hg2+ with different concentrations of 0 µM, 50 µM, 100 µM, 200 µM ( Fig. 5a-b) and 0 µM, 100 µM, 200 µM, 300 µM (Fig. 5c-d) respectively. After dried at room temperature for 5 min and their fluorescence quenching was captured as photo. From the bare CDs @ FP, the emission intensity of CDs at Whatman filter paper (higher grade) is greater when compared to normal filter paper shown in the (Figure. S6). The difference in emission intensity may be due the high pore volume in the normal filter paper leads to absorb the CDs particles on it. On the other hand, Whatman filter paper has low and even surface pore volume which make CDs stays on the surface which leads to high intense emission compared to low grade filter paper, followed by (Table S3) represents the earlier reported probe kits.

The detection of Cr6+ ions with Whatman (higher grade) filter paper shows a better quenching of emission on it, in presence of 100 µM Cr6+ ions solution itself turn off fluorescence is visually noticed followed by at 200 µM the emission were totally quenched. In this case high grade filter paper exhibits good results compared to the low-grade filter paper. On other hand detection attempt of Hg2+ ions display very good visual results with both low and high-grade filter paper.

At 200 µM concentration of Hg2+ ions on both the filter paper with CDs nearly quenched and finally 300 µM on papers entirely turned-off the fluorescence. From the above results obtained from detection of Hg2+ ions with CDs at FP which can reduces the cost of using high grade Whatman filter paper. However, both the probe filter paper shows good results in visual detection of metal ions individually and rapidly. Finally, the CDs in simple low-cost cellulose-based filter paper model detection probe will be efficient, low cost and rapid sensor kit in future.

The photocatalytic degradation ability of CDs over organic dye molecules was extensively studied with the help of cationic dye RhB. On increasing the time duration of photo-irradiation with the mixture, the absorbance value of RhB dye decreased gradually at 554 nm (0–140 min) ( Fig. 6a-b) which was monitored by UV-Vis absorption spectrometer for the time interval of every 20 min. The degradation efficiency rate was calculated from the formula [33].
(3)
Dye degradation rate ( %) = (C0 – C)/C0
Where C and C0 are the absorption value recorded at different time intervals of light irradiation. The degradation efficiency was found that 87 % with the increase in time interval. The reaction rate (k) of the photocatalytic degradation was calculated with the rate constant value of 1.14 × 10−2 min−1 was determined with the help of ln(C / C0) equation from plot (Fig. 6d), with R2 value of 0.9438. The photocatalytic efficiency of the dyes over the catalyst was generally depending on the interaction between the dye molecules with catalyst in aqueous solution and the degradation of dye molecules by CDs are mainly due to the effect of surface state I.e., the small, functional group rich in CDs [34]. From this instance, the photo degradation of the RhB dye over CDs are due the π-π interaction followed surface charge interaction [35], actually, the positively charged dye molecules are greatly attracted towards the negatively charged, tiny sized highly active CDs surface, The CC domain in the CDs core and functional group rich surface are duly attracted over the dye molecule followed by the reaction degradation process happens. The photocatalytic degradation process was initiated by the photo catalyst through in-situ generated reactive species e- and h+ on the surface if CDs [36] in aqueous environment by absorbing the photons from the sunlight. The reactive species were found with the help of various scavengers, for that we have use sodium salt of ethylenediamine tetra-acetic acid Na2-EDTA [37] as holes, terephthalic acid for hydroxyl radical and p-benzoquinone for superoxide were added to the reaction mixture about (1 mM) individually. From these studies Na2-EDTA contained reaction mixture shows reduced in degradation efficiency compared to scavenger free mixture, that is due to generation of holes are the major active species responsible for the degradation of organic dye. However, other trappers did not effectively reduce the degradation process of the dye molecules. Thus, when the light falls on the CDs, photons from the light triggers the electron on the valence band of CDs to conduction band by leaving holes. The holes generate hydroxyl radical (OH•) by reacting water molecules on reaction mixture followed by superoxide radical (O2•−) [38]were generated by O2 molecule react with the electron on CB. Furthermore, the major generated (OH•) by holes are effectively interacted with the RhB dye molecules leads to photo degradation and converted into small molecular fragments and mineral acids as byproducts (Figure. S7a-b). Even though, CDs shows absorbance range within UV-region (Fig. 2(a)) the visible light conservation of CDs was confirmed through excitation dependent emission spectrum (Fig. 2(b)). The multiple functional electron rich groups on the S-CDs like –NH and –OH groups are the active molecules makes better electrostatic interaction with the cationic dye and the optical band gap of ~2.8 eV calculated from Tauc plot (Figure. S7c). CDs not able to degrade the dye completely, it may be due to that bare CDs alone can’t efficiently separate the charge carriers for better results (Table S4) signifies past reported photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye with CDs and their composites. So, combination of CDs with other conventional semiconductor materials can be a better hybrid for water remediation problem. In addition to that the easily synthesized CDs from simple reactants without any other metal-based semiconductors possess very good promising and metal-free photocatalyst for environmental pollutant remediation.

The simple synthesized multi-behavior carbon dots (CDs) with average size of 5 nm were prepared from simple precursors using microwave, which emits bright blue emission with 33 % of quantum yield. The prepared CDs were exhibits excitation independent/dependent emission due to core and molecular surface, it reflects better stability against ionic strength and continuous photobleaching. Fluorescence emission has effectively utilized for detecting the dual metal ions Cr6+ and Hg2+ in aqueous medium with detection limit of 0.09 µM and 0.16 µM through by IFE and PET mechanisms. In addition, CDs were effectively used for assembly of low-cost filter paper for visual detection of both metal ions independently. Apart from the excellent detection capacity, CDs shows band gap of ~2.8 eV with excitation-dependent emission behavior was further facilitated for the sunlight-assisted degradation of RhB dye, as a greener metal-free homogenous catalyst, with the efficiency of 87 % degradation over the time period of 140 min with rate constant of 1.18 × 10−2 min−1. The active species for photocatalytic degradation also studied with the help of radical scavengers under same experimental conditions. Consequently, CDs expressions a very good detection towards metal ions with sensitivity and selectivity followed by degradation. So, the obtained outcomes shows that the simple CDs stand effective to detection and degradation of environmental pollutants in aqueous medium therefore it would be considered as superior environmental material.

Arun Annamalai: Design and Performance of Experiments, Analysis of Experimental Data, Writing – original draft, Kumaresan Annamalai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Ramya Ravichandran: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. S Bharathkumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Sundaravadivel Elumalai: Visualization, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge SRM Institute of Science and Technology for providing the major instrumentation facilities NRC-SRMIST and HRTEM Facility at SCIF set up with support from MNRE (Project No. 31/03/2014-15/PVSE-R&D, Government of India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129800.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
